course: AI Agent Risk, Oversight, and Failure

Claude.notes: Strong opening with Kevin Roose/Sydney as concrete narrative anchor; taxonomy is clean and the five failure categories map cleanly to story exemplars across L1-L4, though insights are often told rather than discovered by a protagonist.
Claude.topIssue: The taxonomy is presented as a finished framework rather than something a character discovers; story scenes (Roose, Cruise, Air Canada) serve as illustrations, not problems the learner solves before the concept arrives.

Gemini.notes: Technically precise: Goodhart's Law, RLHF, underspecification, and the boat-racing CoastRunners example are all stated correctly with proper attribution; Air Canada Moffatt case and Castel sanction details check out.
Gemini.topIssue: The Mata v. Avianca Castel sanction was $5,000 against the lawyers personally — the firm framing is slightly loose; otherwise definitions are crisp and the action-irreversibility framing is well-formed.

Perplexity.notes: Currency holds — Air Canada Moffatt (Feb 2024), Cruise pedestrian-dragging (Oct 2023), and the Mata case are still the canonical references in 2026; lab tasks (classify, design oversight) remain executable against today's deployed agents.
Perplexity.topIssue: Examples cluster in 2023-2024; the module would benefit from a 2025 incident (e.g., agentic browser exploits or the wave of Anthropic computer-use injection demos) given today's June-2026 vantage.

modules.module: M2: Prompt Injection, Goal Misgeneralization, and Emergent Behavior

Claude.notes: Riley Goodside, Simon Willison, and the DeepMind blue-room misgeneralization scenes are vivid and well-chosen, but Goodside and Willison appear once and are dropped — character continuity across the four lessons is weak.
Claude.topIssue: Each lesson reintroduces a fresh cast (Goodside, then DeepMind researchers, then Wei/Schaeffer); no through-line protagonist makes the four failure modes feel like chapters of one investigation.

Gemini.notes: Schaeffer mirage critique, Chinchilla compute-optimal scaling, Wei emergent abilities, sycophancy, and Christiano deceptive alignment are all stated with care and properly nuanced ('contested debate' framing); Andrew Critch principal hierarchy is correctly summarized.
Gemini.topIssue: Kai Greshake's indirect-injection demo is attributed to Saarland University; the original paper was Greshake et al. at CISPA/Saarland — fine, but the proof-of-concept date is closer to early 2023 than vague '2023 PoC' suggests.

Perplexity.notes: Prompt injection content holds up — Willison's framing is still canonical in 2026 — but examples skew 2022-2023 and miss the 2024-2025 wave of agentic browser exploits and computer-use injection demos that practitioners actually face now.
Perplexity.topIssue: No mention of recent agentic-browser injection benchmarks (Anthropic computer use, OpenAI Operator) or the 2025 ChatGPT memory-injection issues that are now the most current real-world referents.

Claude.notes: Air France 447 cockpit and Therac-25 MALFUNCTION 54 openings are tightly engineered to motivate the lesson's framework — the failure to interpret an alarm becomes the exact problem the lesson teaches operators to fix.
Claude.topIssue: Strong narrative-to-concept yoking on L1 and L2, but L3 (scope constraints) opens with abstract framing rather than a scene; the pattern of 'incident → diagnostic framework' breaks here.

Gemini.notes: Therac-25 racetrack-condition framing and BEA Air France 447 conclusions are accurately rendered; HITL position taxonomy and graduated permissions are clean reformulations of standard human-factors literature.
Gemini.topIssue: MALFUNCTION 54 was one of dozens of cryptic codes — the lesson implies operators were specifically told to ignore '54' which slightly overstates the documented record; the broader 'trained to ignore' pattern is accurate.

Perplexity.notes: ED triage and financial-refund lab scenarios are exactly the deployments practitioners are wrestling with in 2026 — the scope-constraint and graduated-autonomy frameworks transfer directly to current LLM-agent products.
Perplexity.topIssue: Reliance on Therac-25 (1985) and Air France 447 (2009) is methodologically sound but feels historical; one current scene (e.g., a 2025 agentic-coding rollback incident) would sharpen currency.

modules.module: M4: Monitoring, Logging, and Detecting Drift

Claude.notes: The Jake Moffatt Air Canada chatbot incident anchors L2 perfectly: 'the logs existed only as retrospective evidence, not as prospective monitoring' is exactly the insight the lesson teaches; Zillow Offers as a monitoring-without-response counter-example is well-placed.
Claude.topIssue: Sydney session-cap story in L1 is told in third person about Microsoft engineers — there is no character whose discovery moment the learner shares.

Gemini.notes: Amazon recruiting algorithm 'stably produces biased outputs is not stable, it is drifted' is a sharp and accurate framing; Zillow $304M Q3 2021 write-down number is correct; Moffatt v. Air Canada BCCRT details (Feb 2024 ruling, retroactive bereavement) check out precisely.
Gemini.topIssue: The lesson treats Amazon's recruiting system as an 'agent' but it was a resume-scoring ML classifier, not an agentic system in the M1 taxonomy sense — the categorization is loose.

Perplexity.notes: Monitoring and logging schema content is the most timeless dimension here — log fields, drift baselines, escalation tiers all map to 2026 LLMOps/AgentOps practice; the claims-triage and legal-research lab scenarios are current.
Perplexity.topIssue: No mention of OpenTelemetry-for-LLMs, Langfuse, Arize Phoenix, or the 2025 wave of agent-observability tooling that practitioners actually deploy today — the lesson is tool-agnostic to a fault.

modules.module: M5: Liability, Accountability, and Organizational Governance

Claude.notes: The Sam Altman board firing in L3 (Nov 17, 2023; 96-hour reversal) is a sharp, dramatic anchor for governance-structure design, but the lab tasks remain abstract policy-design discussions rather than concrete deliverables.
Claude.topIssue: Liability content is dense and accurate but reads more like a legal explainer than a narrative — the learner is not landing the insight through a character's predicament; the disclaimers-don't-survive insight is told, not discovered.

Gemini.notes: Liability theories (vicarious, product, negligence) are correctly applied to AI agents; OpenAI board-governance episode is accurately summarized; RACI framing in L2 is standard and transferable.
Gemini.topIssue: The module asserts US-style liability concepts without flagging EU AI Act (in-force 2024-2026), GDPR Article 22, or the divergent jurisdictional landscape that an Advanced learner needs.

Perplexity.notes: Liability framing is correct but stops at 2023-2024 doctrine; in mid-2026 the EU AI Act high-risk obligations are now active and there is emerging US state-level case law that the module does not engage.
Perplexity.topIssue: No EU AI Act Article 9/10/26 obligations, no Colorado SB 24-205, no 2025 California AI transparency laws — the regulatory currency gap is the most consequential issue for a governance module dated 2026.

Claude.notes: Microsoft Tay opening (sixteen hours to racist outputs, 'no formal adversarial stress-testing protocol') is a clean specification-failure-by-omission scene that motivates the entire framework around evaluation rigor.
Claude.topIssue: L2 and L3 read more like NIST/EU AI Act framework summaries with thin story scaffolding — the Tay momentum from L1 is not sustained through red-teaming and go/no-go lessons.

Gemini.notes: NIST AI RMF and EU AI Act cross-referencing is technically clean; red-team methodology (threat modeling, probing strategy, findings synthesis) maps to industry practice; Tay misclassification as 'not adversarially tested' is fairly characterized.
Gemini.topIssue: Module references NIST and EU AI Act frameworks but does not name specific document IDs (NIST AI 600-1, EU AI Act Annex III) — Advanced learners would benefit from explicit citations.

Perplexity.notes: NIST AI RMF (1.0 released 2023, updated profile 2024) and EU AI Act are correctly identified as the live framework anchors as of 2026; lab tasks (risk taxonomy, red-team design, go/no-go) match current pre-deployment review practice.
Perplexity.topIssue: Tay is a 2016 incident — useful but very stale; a 2025 frontier-model deployment evaluation case (e.g., GPT-5 system card, Claude 4 capability evaluations) would land harder for current learners.

modules.module: M7: Case Studies: High-Profile Agent Failures

Claude.notes: Case-study module shines on narrative density — Amazon recruiting, Microsoft Tay, Knight Capital's 97-errors-per-second-with-no-response-protocol, Air France 447, and Bing Sydney are all consequence-rich incidents that anchor each lesson.
Claude.topIssue: Cases are paired (Amazon+Tay, Knight+Flash Crash, Uber ATG+AF447, ChatGPT+Sydney) which gives narrative variety, but the rapid case-switching means no single protagonist's arc gets to develop across the lesson.

Gemini.notes: Knight Capital 2012 ($440M in 45 minutes, SMARS legacy code, 97 errors/sec) and 2010 Flash Crash details are accurately rendered; Amazon scrapped 2018 after Reuters report is correct; Sydney 'goal drift' reframing from M2's misgeneralization framing is defensible.
Gemini.topIssue: Bing Sydney is framed as 'goal drift' here but as 'goal misgeneralization' in M1/M2 — the same incident gets three slightly different labels across modules without acknowledging the cross-reference.

Perplexity.notes: Cases are historically important but skew old: Amazon recruiting (2014-2018), Knight (2012), Flash Crash (2010), Tay (2016); the only post-2022 case (Sydney/ChatGPT legal hallucinations) is already three-plus years old in mid-2026.
Perplexity.topIssue: Missing the 2024-2025 generation of high-profile agentic failures — DPD swearing chatbot, NYC MyCity legal-advice scandal, Klarna agent rollback, 2025 agentic-coding incidents — which are now the live case literature.

modules.module: M8: Apply It: Conduct a Risk Audit of an Agent in Your Organization

Claude.notes: Capstone module delivers the cleanest applied outcome of the course: lab sequence (scope → threat inventory → oversight gaps → findings report) is a real audit workflow the learner can run against their own agent on Monday.
Claude.topIssue: Lisanne Bainbridge 'Ironies of Automation' (1983) anchor is excellent for the gap-analysis lesson, but the rest of the module relies more on framework than narrative — Bainbridge is the only character whose insight the learner lives through.

Gemini.notes: Bainbridge citation and the 'gap between nominal and real oversight' framing are technically sound; threat inventory and oversight gap analysis methodology is rigorous and maps to standard internal-audit practice.
Gemini.topIssue: Audit framework is presented in isolation from NIST AI RMF and ISO/IEC 42001 (the relevant 2024 standard for AI management systems) — Advanced learners running real audits in 2026 will be expected to map findings to those standards.

Perplexity.notes: The 'audit-your-own-agent' framing is exactly the deliverable practitioners need in mid-2026 as ISO 42001 certifications and EU AI Act high-risk audit obligations come online; lab outputs (scope statement, threat inventory, findings report) are directly usable.
Perplexity.topIssue: Module never names ISO/IEC 42001:2023, EU AI Act conformity assessment procedures, or NIST AI RMF generative profile — the regulatory backbone of real 2026 audits is invisible.
